local_names.hindi: मी पोक ड्राई (बाक चोर मी — चपटे अंडा नूडल्स कीमा पोर्क के साथ)

aliases: 肉脞面 (Bak Chor Mee / Ròu Cuò Miàn)
मी पोक ड्राई (बाक चोर मी — चपटे अंडा नूडல्स कीमा पोर्क के साथ)

english: 肉脞面 (Bak Chor Mee / Ròu Cuò Miàn)
hindi: मी पोक ड्राई (बाक चोर मी — चपटे अंडा नूडल्स कीमा पोर्क के साथ)

region_of_origin: Chaoshan (Teochew), China — popularised as a Singapore Teochew hawker signature
food_subgroup: Flat egg noodles tossed in lard, vinegar, dark soy, and chilli with minced pork mixture

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no concept for Mee Pok Dry (Bak Chor Mee). Closest parent is a tossed flat egg noodle dish with minced meat sauce. No exact FoodOn URI available; null is used honestly.
ontology.foodex2_description: Flat egg noodles tossed in lard, dark soy, vinegar, and chilli sauce with minced pork, shiitake mushroom, pork liver, and meatballs — closest FoodEx2 parent for composite noodle dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Bak Chor Mee. A0CFA (composite noodle dish) is used as the closest defensible parent code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to this specific hawker noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Mee Pok Dry weighs approximately 380g, comprising roughly 150g flat egg noodles, 150g minced pork mixture (including liver, meatballs, and mushrooms), and 80g of the signature sauce (dark soy, vinegar, chilli, and lard).

nutrient_source.title: Mee Pok Dry / Bak Chor Mee — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Mee Pok Dry / Bak Chor Mee. All core macros and micronutrients are taken from this verified entry. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component data for constituent ingredients (egg noodles, pork, pork liver, shiitake mushroom) where HPB does not report these micronutrients directly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — egg noodles and composite noodle dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Chor Mee. GI of 60 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for egg noodles (GI ~55–65, medium category). The presence of black vinegar (acetic acid) in the sauce has a documented GI-lowering effect (Johnston et al., 2004, EJCN). High protein from pork and liver and fat from lard further moderate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (60 × 62.7g) / 100 ≈ 37.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (550mg/100g); a standard 380g plate delivers approximately 2,090mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000mg in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid Mee Pok Dry or request the sauce served on the side to limit dark soy, chilli, and vinegar sauce quantity; the concentrated sauce mixture is the primary sodium driver and can be substantially reduced by requesting less sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (60) with a high glycemic load per serving (37.6) due to the large noodle portion; however, black vinegar (acetic acid) in the sauce has a documented postprandial glucose-moderating effect, and high protein and fat content further buffer glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Mee Pok Dry in moderation by choosing a smaller portion (200–250g), retaining the vinegar-based sauce which attenuates postprandial glucose rise, and pairing with additional vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (3.5g/100g) from rendered pork lard, high cholesterol (95mg/100g) from pork liver and lard, and very high sodium (550mg/100g) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors in this dish.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 200g or less, request reduced lard and sauce, and note that the pork liver contributes beneficial iron, B12, folate, and selenium alongside its cholesterol load; overall frequency of consumption should be moderated to once weekly or less.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork liver provides exceptional anti-anaemia micronutrients (iron 3.2mg/100g, B12 2.8mcg/100g, folate 45mcg/100g, selenium 25mcg/100g, vitamin A 185mcg RAE/100g); however, rendered lard contributes saturated fat (3.5g/100g) which may promote systemic inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the liver-derived micronutrients in this dish but should moderate consumption frequency due to the pro-inflammatory saturated fat from lard; shiitake mushrooms in the dish provide anti-inflammatory ergothioneine as an additional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mee Pok Dry, also known as Bak Chor Mee (肉脞麵乾), is one of Singapore's most iconic Teochew hawker noodle dishes. It features flat, wide yellow egg noodles (mee pok) that are blanched briefly in boiling water and then tossed vigorously in a signature sauce comprising rendered pork lard, black vinegar, dark soy sauce, and chilli paste. The dish is topped with a savoury mixture of seasoned minced pork (bak chor), sliced pork liver, pork meatballs, reconstituted dried shiitake mushrooms, fish cake slices, and spring onion. A small bowl of clear pork-bone broth — often containing fish balls or wontons — is served alongside for sipping. The flavour profile is complex: tangy and sour from the vinegar, rich and umami from the lard and dark soy, spicy from the chilli, and deeply savoury from the pork components. Nutritionally, this dish is notable for its exceptional micronutrient density from pork liver: it delivers 185mcg RAE vitamin A, 2.8mcg vitamin B12, 45mcg folate, 3.2mg iron, and 25mcg selenium per 100g — making it one of the most micronutrient-rich hawker dishes available. The black vinegar in the sauce has a documented glycaemic-moderating effect via acetic acid, giving the dish a lower postprandial glucose impact than its carbohydrate content alone would suggest. The primary clinical concern is sodium: at 550mg/100g, a standard 380g plate delivers approximately 2,090mg sodium, exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit in a single meal. The dish is dairy-free and contains eggs, gluten, soy, and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: mee pok dry vs soup nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros and primary micronutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean hawker dishes. The entry is an exact match for Mee Pok Dry / Bak Chor Mee. Minor micronutrients (copper, manganese, vitamin K) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component data for constituent ingredients (egg noodles, pork, pork liver, shiitake mushroom) where HPB does not report these micronutrients directly. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodles, which is the closest approved GI source available.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite hawker dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g is set to -1.0: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database. These three fields (-1.0) reflect genuine data unavailability from approved sources. Copper (0.08mg/100g), manganese (0.1mg/100g), and vitamin K (8.0mcg/100g) values are supplemented estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data for egg noodles, pork, pork liver, and shiitake mushroom in proportions consistent with the dish composition; these carry confidence 0.9. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Bak Chor Mee exists in the published literature. GL per serving = (60 × 62.7g available carbohydrate in 380g serving) / 100 = 37.6. The dish is not halal due to pork content.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (198.0), protein_g (11.5), fat_g (10.2), and carbohydrate_g (16.5) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo — the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. nutrient_source is fully populated.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.8), sodium_mg (550.0), saturated_fat_g (3.5), and sugar_g (2.5) are all present as numeric values consistent with the HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (60), gi_category ('medium'), glycemic_load_per_serving (37.6), and gi_source are all present. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for egg noodles, which is the approved GI source. Estimation basis is fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 code A0CFA is used as the closest defensible parent for a composite noodle dish with gap note. FoodOn URI is null with honest gap note explaining no exact concept exists. SNOMED CT is null with gap note. No codes are invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters (approximately 1,450 characters). It covers what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (sodium, GI, liver micronutrients), and key nutrients. common_user_queries, meal_context, and ingredient_synonyms are all populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions are present: Hypertension (high concern — sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern — medium GI with vinegar buffering), and Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern — saturated fat, cholesterol, sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and a specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is correctly set to 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Self-check performed: every string value in aliases_by_language has been verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. aliases_by_language english (18 entries), chinese (8 entries), malay (3 entries), tamil (2 entries), hindi (2 entries), romanized (4 entries) — all 34 unique strings confirmed present in flat aliases array. Comprehensive coverage of English variants, Chinese script forms (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, romanized forms, abbreviations (BCM, BCM dry), and regional spelling variants.
